titleStmt.title: Stripe order in the doped Hubbard model on the honeycomb lattice

funder.orgName: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality

National Key Research and Development Program of China

affiliation.orgName: Key Laboratory of Artificial Structures and Quantum Control (Ministry of Education)

analytic.title: Stripe order in the doped Hubbard model on the honeycomb lattice

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We study the ground state properties of the doped Hubbard model with strong interactions on honeycomb lattice by the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) method. At halffilling, due to the absence of minus sign problem, it is now well established by large-scale Quantum Monte Carlo calculations that a Dirac semi-metal to anti-ferromagnetic Mott insulator transition occurs with the increase of the interaction strength U for the Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice. However, an understanding of the fate of the anti-ferromagnetic Mott insulator when holes are doped into the system is still lacking. In this work, by calculating the local spin and charge density for width-4 cylinders with DMRG, we discover a half-filled stripe order in the doped Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice. We also perform complementary large-scale mean-field calculations with renormalized interaction strength. We observe half-filled stripe order and find stripe states with filling close to one half are nearly degenerate in energy.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: The Hubbard model is one of the fundamental model systems in the exploration of quantum many-body effect [1]. It captures many important aspects of correlated electrons by considering only the on-site Coulomb interactions. The Hubbard model on square lattice is related to the copper oxide superconductors [2][3][4][5] and has been extensively studied numerically [6]. The Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice, which has a similar structure as square lattice, is also widely studied, partly for its connection to graphene [7]. It is also a playing ground to study the correlation-driven metal-insulator transition [8]. The properties of the Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice at half-filling can be accurately determined by Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) method due to the absence of the minus-sign problem on bipartite lattices [9]. The ground state phase diagram at half-filling is now well established with large-scale QMC calculations [9][10][11]. At half-filling, in contrast to the Hubbard model on square lattice where there is no metal-insulator transition at finite interaction strength [12], a transition occurs from the Dirac semi-metal phase at weak interactions to the Mott insulator phase at strong interactions on the honeycomb lattice. The critical interaction strength is estimated to be U c ≈ 3.8 [11,13] by large-scale QMC calculations with careful finite-size analysis. The transition is found to be in the Gross-Neveu-Yukawa [11,13] universality class. Anti-ferromagnetic (AF) long-range order is also found to develop accompanying the Mott transition [9][10][11]. In the large interaction strength limit at half-filling, the effective low energy Heisenberg model on honeycomb lattice is known to order anti-ferromanetically with local momentum m = 0.2677 (6) [14,15].
The fate of AF Mott insulator if holes are doped in the * qinmingpu@sjtu.edu.cn

Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice is still unknown. Away from half-filling, the infamous minus sign problem emerges which hampers the application of QMC to large system sizes and low temperature [16,17]. The competition between kinetic and potential energies can lead to exotic states when holes are introduced into the Mott insulator [3]. In cuprates, where the CuO plane has a square lattice structure, high-T c superconductivity can emerge by doping holes into the parent antiferromagnetic Mott insulator [18]. Stripe order [19] is also observed in the phase diagram of cuprates. For the Hubbard model on square lattice, which is believed to be relevant to cuprates, different ground states are obtained [20][21][22]. A recent work by a collaboration of state-ofart numerical approaches [23] shows the ground state of the Hubbard model on square lattice has stripe order in the under-doped region with strong interactions. Results also indicate stripe order competes with possible d-wave superconducting order on square lattice [24]. Given the similarity of honeycomb and square lattice, e.g., both of them are bipartite and order anti-ferromagnetically in the strong interaction region at half filling, it is natural to ask whether stripe order also exists on the honeycomb lattice when holes are doped into the AF Mott insulator phase.

Many attempts have been made trying to reveal the properties of the doped Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice. Substantial attentions have been paid to the 1/4 doping case, where the density of states displays a Van Hove singularity and the Fermi surface has a nesting feature. At weak interaction, which is relevant to graphene [7], d + id superconductivity was found in the Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice near 1/4 doping by variational Monte Carlo [25], renormalization group [26][27][28][29], singular-mode functional renormalization group and variational Monte Carlo [30], and by a combination of different numerical methods [31]. Spontaneous quantum Hall effect is also found at 1/4 doping [30][31][32]. In a recent work, p + ip superconductivity was obtained with Grass- mann tensor product state approach [33] in the infinite-U limit, i.e., the t-J model. A detailed analysis of the possible pairing symmetry can be found in [34].

Experimentally, real materials with honeycomb structure other than graphene [7] were also synthesized. Longrange AF Neel order was observed in real materials with honeycomb structure, Na 2 IrO 3 [35] and InCu 2 3 V 1 3 O 3 [36] for examples. Superconductivity was also discovered in the pnictide SrPtAs which has a honeycomb structure [37]. Time-reversal symmetry was also found to be broken in the superconducting state of SrPtAs [38,39]. A pressure-driven superconductivity in FePSe 3 , which has an iron-based honeycomb lattice structure was reported recently [40].

In this work we study the ground state properties of the doped Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice in the strong interaction region, i.e., in the AF Mott insulator phase. We employ the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) method with which reliable results of ground state are obtained. We discover a half-filled stripe order at 1/16 doping, which is similar as the stripe state on the square lattice [41]. We also perform large-scale meanfield calculations with renormalized interaction strength for large systems. We find half-filled stripe state in the mean-field calculations. Stripe state with filling other one half are also obtained and the energies for stripe states near half filling are almost degenerate in meanfield level which indicates the wave-length of stripe can fluctuate without causing much energy, a phenomenon also observed in the square lattice case [23].

div.head: II. MODEL AND METHODS
div.p: The Hamiltonian of Hubbard model is:
div.formula: H = -t i,j ,σ c † iσ c jσ + U i n i↑ n i↓(1)
div.p: where c i,σ , c † i,σ and n iσ = c † iσ c iσ are the annihilation, creation and density operators on site i for spin species σ. i, j denotes the nearest neighboring hopping on the honeycomb lattice. U > 0 is the repulsive interaction strength and t is the hopping constant which is set to the energy unit. The total number of electrons with spin σ(σ =↑, ↓) are denoted by N σ . We only consider the spin-balanced case so the total number of electrons is N e = 2N σ . We denote the number of primitive cells in the studied system as N = L 1 × L 2 (see the left panel of Fig. 1), so the total number of sites is 2N because each primitive cell contains two sites. The doping level is h = N hole 2N . where N hole = 2N -N e is the total number of holes. The local spin and hole density at site i is
div.formula: S i = (n i,↑ -n i,↓ )/2 and h i = (1 -n i,↑ -n i,↓ ) respectively.
div.p: In DMRG calculations, we adopt cylinder geometry, i.e., periodic (open) boundary conditions along L 1 (L 2 ) directions, while periodic boundary conditions in both directions are used in mean-field calculations [42].
To characterize the stripe structure, staggered spin density which is defined as (-1) i S i is plotted.

DMRG [44,45] is an extremely accurate method and arguably the workhorse for the study of one-dimensional chains and narrow cylinders [46,47]. In DMRG calculation, we focus on 1/16 doping and U = 8. We calculate 4 × 8 and 4 × 16 systems which contain 64 and 128 sites respectively. The state kept in the DMRG calculation is as large as m = 9000 to make sure the linear scaling region is reached and linear extrapolations with truncation error for physical quantities are performed. To complement the DMRG study of width-4 cylinders, we also perform large-scale mean-field calculation on systems with larger size. It is known that mean-field calculation usually exaggerates the order parameter [48], so in the mean-field calculation we choose a smaller renormalized interaction strength U ≈ 3.0 (above the critical interaction strength U mf c ≈ 2.23 in the mean-field phase diagram [49]). Details of the mean-field calculations can be found in the Appendix [43].

div.head: A. DMRG
div.p: In DMRG calculation, we rearrange the honeycomb lattice into a square lattice with next nearest neighboring interactions (see the right panel of Fig. 1) and map the square lattice to a one-dimensional chain in a common manner [50]. In Fig. 2 we plot the local hole and staggered spin density for the 4×8 system at 1/16 doping with U = 8. We apply anti-ferromagnetic pinning fields with strength h p = 0.5 on the open edges of the cylinder to break the SU(2) symmetry explicitly. In this way, we can measure the local spin density to probe the possible spin order instead of the spin-spin correlation function, which is more difficult to calculate [51]. Color maps and plots for both the hole and staggered density along L 2 direction are shown in Fig. 2. DMRG results with both finite kept state m (from 7000 to 9000) and the extrapolated to zero truncation error results are presented [51]. In the Appendix [43], we show the scaling of energy and hole density with truncation error and find both of them enter the linear scaling region which ensures the reliabil- ity of the extrapolation.
In Fig. 3 we study a longer cylinder with size 4 × 16 and U = 8 also at 1/16 doping. Same as in Fig. 2, color map and plots for both the hole and staggered density along L 2 direction are shown.

From both Fig. 2 and Fig. 3, we can find a periodic oscillation of both the hole and staggered spin density. The wave-length of staggered spin density is twice that of hole density. At the peak position of hole density, where holes are concentrated, the spin density shows a π phase shift which is the feature of the stripe order [19]. The stripe order is half-filled because there are 2(4) stripes and 4(8) holes in Fig. 2 (Fig. 3), and the width of the system is 4. The amplitude of hole modulation on honeycomb lattice is smaller than that on the square lattice [23,48] because of the larger quantum fluctuation on honeycomb lattice [52].

The fast increase of the number of required kept states in DMRG with width of the system prevents us to reach cylinder beyond width 4. In general, the quantum fluctuation is larger for narrow cylinders as shown in the evolution of AF order in Heisenberg model from one to two dimension. The stripe order was found to increase in wider cylinders on square lattice [23,48]. Nevertheless, we explore the boundary (pinning field) effect in the Appendix [43]. We find the stripe order in the bulk of the system is robust against the decrease of the strength of pinning fields.

It is worth noting that the stripe order in honeycomb lattice is in the diagonal direction of the underlying square lattice if we arrange the honeycomb lattice in the brick-wall way. The diagonal stripe state was found to be in the low energy manifold of the t -J model on square lattice [53] with tensor network states method.

div.head: B. Mean Field
div.p: It is known that for the doped Hubbard model on square lattice, mean-field calculations give stripe order in the ground state [54][55][56][57]. We perform similar mean- field calculations for the honeycomb lattice [58]. At halffilling, the critical interaction strength which separate the semi-metal to the AF insulator phase is U mf c ≈ 2.23 [49] (see also in the Appendix [43]).
div.note: 1 KROH 1 KROH 1 KROH +ROHGHQVLW\ 1 KROH 6SLQGHQVLW\ 1 KROH 1 KROH
div.p: Instead of using U = 8 as in the DMRG calculation, we choose a renormalized interaction strength with U ≈ 3 which is above U mf c ≈ 2.23, to ensure the ground state is in the Neel order phase before holes are doped. The effect of interaction strength on the mean-field results is discussed in the Appendix [43]. In Fig. 4, local hole density are plotted on 16 × 16 lattices with U = 3 and for a serial of dopings. With the increase of doping, the distribution of the holes in the system changes significantly which was also observed in the square lattice case [58]. At low doping, the pattern shows the symmetry of the lattice. These patterns at low density are basically from finite size effects and they will change with the increase of system size [58]. A half-filled stripe order at N hole = 32 which corresponding to 1/16 doping can be found in Fig. 4.

In Fig. 5 we show the mean-field results for the 8 × 48 lattice with different number of holes and U = 3. For N hole = 8, 24, and 48, states with 2, 6, and 12 stripes can be seen from Fig. 5. Again, all of the stripes are halffilled. We also show plots for both the local hole and staggered spin density along L y direction for N hole = 24 in Fig. 5, where the periodic modulation of them can be seen. The sharp peak in the hole density plot means holes are very concentrated, while in the DMRG results, hole density shows a more smooth modulation. We also notice in the DMRG results for cylinders in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3, the hole density at the boundaries is much less than the minimum value of the oscillation of hole in the bulk. We also perform a mean-field calculation for the same cylinder in Fig. 3 and find a similar phenomenon in the mean-field results which indicates open boundary tends to push holes to the bulk.

From these results, we know mean-field gives an insulator stripe state where holes are nearly localized in one direction of the lattice, while in the real many-body ground state as in the DMRG results in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3, holes are more dispersed and is more helpful for the development of superconductivity.

Other than the half-filled stripe state, we also find stripe state with filling close to 1/2, 1/3 and 2/5 for examples, in the mean-field calculations. However, the energies of these stripe states are nearly degenerate which indicates the wave-length of the stripe order can fluctuate without causing much energy as in the square lattice case [23]. The details can be found in the Appendix [43].

div.head: IV. SUMMARY AND PERSPECTIVES
div.p: We discover the half-filled stripe order in the ground state of the doped Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice in the strong interaction region by studying width-4 cylinders with DMRG. We also carry out complementary mean-field calculations on large systems with renormalized interaction strength and find the half-filled stripe order. In the mean-field calculations, the stripe states in the vicinity of half-filling are nearly degenerate in energy, which indicates the wave-length of stripe can fluctuate freely as in the square lattice case [23]. Our results indicate that the similarity in real space between the square and honeycomb lattice plays the dominant role in the strong interaction region given the huge difference between the Fermi-surfaces of them. There are real materials [59] with honeycomb lattice structure, where the stripe order could be possibly measured with neutron scattering [60] or scanning tunneling microscope [61] techniques. The stripe order could also be observed on artificially synthesized systems with honeycomb structure [62]. The role of stripe order to the possible superconducting pairing order of the doped Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice is a question one may ask. The presence of stripe order means in the calculation of pairing order or correlation, unit cell compatible to the stripe order needs to be chosen in order not to frustrate the stripe order. How the stripe order melts with thermal fluctuation [41] is an interesting question since experiments are performed at finite temperature. Whether stripe order is a universal consequence of doping anti-ferromagnetic Mott insulator is an interesting topic for future investigation.

div.head: Appendix A: The convergence of DMRG results
div.p: In this section, we check the convergence of results with the kept state m in the DMRG calculations. In Fig. 6 and Fig. 7, we plot the scaling of energy and the peak value of hole density versus the truncation error for 4 × 8 and 4 × 16 systems respectively. A linear fit using the five points with the largest m (smallest truncation error ) is performed. The largest m are 9000 and 7000 for 4 × 8 and 4 × 16 respectively. As we can see from Fig. 6 and Fig. 7, both energy and peak value of hole density reach the linear scaling region in our calculation, which ensures the reliability of the extrapolation to zero truncation error. Eq. (C1) is block-diagonal:
div.formula: H MF = H M F ↑ H M F ↓
div.p: Hence we can diagonalize the block matrix H M F σ (σ =↑, ↓) independently for given n iσ (σ =↑, ↓). The ground state is determined by solving the Hamiltonian selfconsistently. 10: Extrapolated DMRG and mean-field results of the hole and spin density for a 4 × 16 cylinder with 8 holes. Pinning fields with strength hp = 0.5 are applied at the boundaries. The DMRG results are for U = 8 while the mean-field results are with an effective U = 3. Upper (Lower): plots of hole (staggered spin) density along L2 direction. We can see even with a smaller effective U value, the stripe order in mean-field is larger than DMRG results. However, the stripe structure is the same. We first randomly select initial values for n iσ (σ =↑ , ↓). Then we diagonalize the Hamiltonian in Eq. (C1), from which a wavefucntion of N σ particles are obtained. We can calculate the local density from this wavefucntion, which is then used to update the Hamiltonian. This process is repeated untill the density is convergent.

The convergence is not guaranteed if the density calculated from last step wave-function is taken as input for the current step. To improve convergence, we take advantage of a technique called Anderson mixing [65], which has widely usage in electronic structure computations. The wave-function could converge to a local minimum, if we start from random initial densities. To make sure the global minimum is reached, additional strategies are adopted. We try different initial densities and the global minimum is determined as the one with lowest energy. We also add small perturbation to the converged wavefunction, and then use it as new initial densities for the self-consistent iteration. This procedure is repeated several times.

figure.head: 5 FIG. 1 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1: Left: sketch of the honeycomb lattice. Blue and red dots represent sites for the two sub-lattices respectively. We choose the two primitive vectors as v1 and v2. The dotted rhombus contains a L1 × L2 = 4 × 4 super-cell. Right: in DMRG calculation, we arrange the super-cell into a square lattice which is then mapped into a one-dimensional chain in the ordinary manner. The 4 × 4 cell in the left panel is transformed into a square lattice with size 4 × 8.

figure.head: FIG. 2 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2: DMRG results of the hole and spin density for a 4 × 8 cylinder with U = 8 and 4 holes. Top: color map of the hole (left) and spin density (right). Middle (Bottom): plots of hole (staggered spin) density along L2 direction. Both results with finite kept state m and the extrapolated to zero truncation error results are shown. The details of the extrapolation can be found in the Appendix [43].

figure.head: FIG. 3 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3: Similar as Fig. 2. The system is a 4 × 16 cylinder with U = 8 and 8 holes.

figure.head: FIG. 4 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4: Color map of mean filed results of hole density on a 16×16 system with different dopings and U = 3. Half-filled stripe order is developed at N hole = 32 which corresponds to 1/16 doping.

figure.head: FIG. 5 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 5: Mean field results for 8×48 system with U=3. Top: color map of hole (left) and spin density (right) for N hole = 8, 24, and 48. Middle (Bottom): plots of hole (staggered spin) density along L2 direction with N hole = 24.

figure.head: FIG. 8 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 8: DMRG results of the hole and spin density for a 4 × 16 cylinder with U = 8 and 8 holes. Upper (Lower): plots of hole (staggered spin) density along L2 direction with kept state m = 5000. Results for boundary pinning fields with strengths hp = 0.5, 0.25, and 0.125 are shown. We can find the strength of pinning fields only affects the results near the boundaries.

FIG. 9: Mean-filed results for ground state energy per site, double occupancy, single particle gap, and staggered magnetization at half filling in the thermaldynamic limit. We can see a metal-insulator transition at U ≈ 2.23.

figure.head: FIG. 11 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 11: Color map of hole density on a series of lattices with sizes 24 × 24, 28 × 28, 32 × 32, and 36 × 36 at U = 3.16, and with N hole = 24, 28, 32, and 36 respectively.

figure.head: FIG. 12 :FIG. 13 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 12: Color map of hole density on a 16 × 16 lattice with N hole = 16 and U = 3.1, 3.16, 3.2, 3.28, and 3.3.

Scaling of energy and the peak value of hole density versus the truncation error in DMRG for the 4 × 8 cylinder. The fit is performed using the last 5 points. The largest state kept M is 9000 (the last point). Upper: energy. Lower: peak hole density.

row.cell: [62] M. Polini, F. Guinea, M. Lewenstein, H. C. Manoharan,
[63] M. Fishman, S. R. White, and E. M. Stoudenmire, The
ITensor software library for tensor network calculations
[64] A. W. Sandvik and H. G. Evertz, Phys. Rev. B 82,

figure.figDesc: Scaling of energy and the peak value of hole density versus the truncation error in DMRG for 4 × 16 cylinder. The fit is performed using the last 5 points. The largest state kept M is 7000 (the last point). Upper: energy. Lower: peak hole density.

div.p: Acknowledgments M. Q. thank C.-M. Chung, Schollwöck, S. R. White and S. Zhang for earlier collaborations on related topic. X.Y. and H. Z. acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China ((Grants No. 92065201, Grants No. 11790313, No. 11674226, No. 11861161003, No. 11521404, No. 11634009, No. 11674222, No. 11874256, No. U1632102, and No. 11874258), National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grants No. 2016YFA0300403, No. 2016YFA0301003), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (Grants No. 19JC1412701, No. 2019SHZDZX01). M. Q. is supported by a start-up fund from School of Physics and Astronomy in Shanghai Jiao Tong University. M. Q. also acknowledge the support of computational resources by S. Zhang at the Flatiron Institute. The DMRG calculations in this work are performed with the iTensor package [63].

div.head: Appendix B: The boundary effects
div.p: We study the effect of the strength of pinning fields at boundaries for a 4 × 16 cylinder with fixed kept state m = 5000 in Fig. 8. As we can see in Fig. 8, by decreasing the strength of pinning fields, the hole density remains nearly unchanged. The spin density near the boundaries decrease while the "bulk" value doesn't change. This result indicates the stripe order in the bulk is robust against boundary effect.

div.head: Appendix C: Mean-field calculation
div.p: We break the Hamiltonian of Hubbard model in the following form in the mean-field calculation:
where n iσ (σ =↑, ↓) is the density on site i which needs to be determined self-consistently. Because spin degrees of freedom are decoupled, the mean-field Hamiltonian in

We can also test whether a certain type of stripe state is the ground state by using it as the initial wavefunction. After we obtain a stripe state on small lattices, we can construct a wave-function on large lattice to test whether it is also the solution for large system.

div.head: Half-filling case
div.p: In Fig. 9 we show the mean field results for ground state energy, double occupancy, single particle gap, and AF magnetization at half filling in the thermaldynamic limit. From Fig. 9, we can see a second-order metalinsulator transition occurs at U ≈ 2.23 at half-filling.

div.head: Comparison with DMRG results
div.p: In Fig. 10 we show a comparison of the DMRG (extrapolated to zero truncation error) and mean-field results for a 4 × 16 cylinder at 1/16 doping with pining field h m = 0.5 applied at the boundaries. The DMRG results are for U = 8 while a reduced effective interaction U = 3 is used in the mean-field calculation. From Fig. 10 we can see the stripe order from DMRG is weaker than the mean-field results, which is a manifestation of the quantum fluctuation. However, from the DMRG results we can conclude the stripe order is not killed by quantum fluctuation. We also notice in both results, the hole den-sity at boundaries is much less than the minimum value in the bulk, which means the open boundaries have a effect of pushing holes to the bulk and increase the average hole densities in the bulk for finite system.

div.head: The effect of U
div.p: In Fig. 11 we show the ground states for large system sizes from mean-field calculations. As we can see, all of them are half-filled stripe states. The stripe state can be only obtained with U > 3. Fig. 12 shows the effect of U on 16×16 lattice. As we can see, stripe states is only found for U ranging from 3.1 to 3.3, which also explains why we do not find stripe at N hole = 16 in Fig. 4 in the main text.

div.head: Stripe states with different fillings
div.p: In Fig. 13, we study lattice with size 20×40 compatible for stripe states with both 1/2 and 2/5 filling, lattice with size 12 × 30 compatible for stripe states with both 1/2 and 1/3 filling, and lattice with size 30 × 40 compatible for stripe states with both 2/5 and 1/3 filling. In all the cases, both stripes state are stable in the mean-filed calculation and we find they are almost degenerate in energy.

monogr.title: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering Sciences
